preamble: The shift from a voiced velar fricative [ɣ] to a voiceless uvular plosive [q] represents a process of fortition and devoicing. This often occurs in loanword adaptation or as a result of phonological pressure to maintain distinctiveness among back consonants.

examples.note: In some languages adopting Arabic words, the ghayn [ɣ] is adapted as a voiceless uvular stop [q] to match local phonotactics.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Watson, J. C. (2002). The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic.
